refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and alert propagation to enable rapid human intervention.
DP7: Combine DP1 + DP3 + DP6 as an integrated availability architecture: redundancy for masking, predictive maintenance for prevention, diagnostics for rapid recovery.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) predictive condition monitoring to prevent faults, and (3) comprehensive fault detection and diagnostics to enable rapid recovery and restoration.

potential_sources: Redundancy (DP1 component) and predictive maintenance (DP3 component) both depend on shared sensor/telemetry infrastructure—risk of common-mode failure if sensor suite is not itself redundant.

Failover logic and diagnostics (DP6 component) must coordinate state synchronization; tight coupling between redundancy controller and fault detection system could create race conditions or cascading failures if not carefully architected.
Predictive models (DP3) require historical data and calibration; tight coupling to specific operational profiles may reduce transferability across different worksites or customer configurations.
Graceful degradation (DP4) is not explicitly included in DP7; if core functions cannot be suspended, availability gains from redundancy alone may be insufficient to meet 0.97 target.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is logically sound and addresses the three pillars of availability (prevention, masking, recovery). However, the integration introduces coordination complexity. The mapping is acceptable if architectural boundaries clearly separate redundancy control, predictive analytics, and diagnostics into loosely coupled modules with well-defined interfaces. Risk: over-coupling these three concerns into a monolithic controller would violate independence and increase failure modes.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be kept in synchronized state without excessive overhead. Risk: Synchronization latency or data inconsistency could negate failover benefits.

Assumption: Predictive maintenance models are accurate and calibrated for the target operational environment. Risk: Model drift or environmental variability could cause false positives (unnecessary maintenance) or false negatives (missed faults).

Assumption: Fault detection and diagnostics can identify root causes fast enough to enable human intervention within acceptable recovery time windows. Risk: Complex faults or cascading failures may exceed diagnostic latency budgets.

Assumption: Customer-defined target of 0.97 availability is achievable with the proposed architecture given realistic component MTBF and MTTR values. Risk: If component reliability is poor or repair logistics are slow, redundancy alone may not suffice.

Assumption: Worksite safety plan and operational policies do not impose constraints that conflict with automated failover or graceful degradation (e.g., safety interlocks that prevent operation in degraded mode). Risk: Safety requirements may force system shutdown instead of continued operation, reducing effective availability.

Assumption: Telemetry and sensor infrastructure supporting redundancy and predictive maintenance is itself highly available. Risk: If monitoring infrastructure fails, both prevention and masking capabilities degrade.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP7 to explicitly include: (1) Redundancy scope definition—which subsystems are redundant and which are single-point-of-failure (justified by risk/cost trade-off); (2) Sensor/telemetry redundancy strategy to prevent common-mode failure of monitoring infrastructure; (3) Graceful degradation policy—define which functions can be suspended and under what fault conditions; (4) Synchronization and failover latency budget—specify maximum acceptable downtime during failover; (5) Predictive model validation and recalibration strategy to manage model drift. Additionally, map DP7 to candidate IMs (e.g., redundancy controller module, predictive analytics service, diagnostics engine) to expose architectural coupling and ownership boundaries. Validate that the 0.97 availability target is achievable by combining realistic component MTBF/MTTR with the proposed architecture using availability models (e.g., Markov chains or fault tree analysis).

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for availability architecture encompassing redundancy management, predictive condition monitoring, fault detection, diagnostics, and recovery orchestration across all critical subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound sensor and subsystem health data streams for condition monitoring and fault detection

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound alerts and diagnostic reports triggered by fault detection or predictive anomalies

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional control signals for failover activation, subsystem switching, and recovery directives

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for distributed redundancy coordination and remote diagnostics

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: availability, fault tolerance, and diagnostics are core safety concerns. Redundancy management, predictive monitoring, and recovery orchestration align directly with safety service responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only requires inbound telemetry and outbound alerts/tasking.

Moderate fit: NGHE is a top-level solution container but lacks specificity for availability architecture. Availability logic would diffuse across multiple subsystems within NGHE. Redundancy and failover would require coordination with Vehicle and Teleoperation, creating tight coupling and unclear ownership.

Weak fit: Vehicle is a physical/operational domain module. Availability architecture is a cross-cutting concern that must coordinate redundancy across Vehicle subsystems, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Placing it in Vehicle creates artificial coupling and obscures safety-critical logic.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a functional domain (remote control capability). Availability architecture is orthogonal to teleoperation operations. Placing redundancy and fault detection here would diffuse safety-critical logic into a non-safety module and create inappropriate dependencies.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the natural architectural home for integrated availability architecture because: (1) redundancy, fault tolerance, and rapid recovery are inherently safety-critical concerns; (2) predictive condition monitoring and diagnostics serve to prevent unsafe states; (3) the module already owns safety-related decision logic and alert generation; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking out) align with safety service responsibilities; (5) ownership is unambiguous and avoids diffusion across operational domains.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services may become a monolithic module if availability architecture grows to include domain-specific redundancy strategies (e.g., Vehicle propulsion failover vs. Teleoperation link failover). Mitigation: decompose into sub-modules (Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine) within Safety Services boundary.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring requires deep subsystem knowledge (Vehicle sensors, Teleoperation link quality). Mitigation: establish clear telemetry contracts; Safety Services consumes standardized health metrics, not raw subsystem internals.
Risk: Failover tasking may conflict with ongoing Vehicle or Teleoperation operations. Mitigation: define priority rules and handoff protocols in Tasking interface specification.

Risk: Network Link dependency for distributed redundancy coordination introduces single point of failure. Mitigation: ensure redundancy architecture itself is resilient to network partitions (local failover capability).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: STRONG. Acceptance criterion (measured availability ≥ 0.97 over agreed evaluation period) is quantifiable and directly traceable to Safety Services module outputs. Telemetry inbound interface provides observability; SafetyAlert and Tasking outbound interfaces enable verification of fault detection, diagnostics, and recovery orchestration. Network Link supports distributed redundancy validation. Verification can be conducted through: (1) availability metrics aggregation from Safety Services logs, (2) failover activation audit trails, (3) predictive anomaly detection accuracy assessment, (4) recovery time measurement against SLAs.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: EXCELLENT. Safety Services is unambiguously assigned as the owner of integrated availability architecture. The module boundary is well-defined with clear responsibility for redundancy management, predictive condition monitoring, fault detection, diagnostics, and recovery orchestration. External interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Network Link) establish clean contract boundaries. Ownership avoids diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or top-level NGHE Solution containers. Worksite safety plan and operational policies can be enforced through Safety Services decision logic and alert generation.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 (Integrated availability architecture) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (achieve and maintain operational availability ≥ target threshold). The three pillars of DP7—(1) redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) predictive condition monitoring to prevent faults, (3) comprehensive fault detection and diagnostics—form a coherent, non-redundant solution strategy. Redundancy and failover provide fault tolerance; predictive monitoring and diagnostics enable proactive prevention and rapid recovery. Together, these mechanisms are necessary and sufficient to sustain availability above target. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: MODERATE RISK: Predictive condition monitoring requires standardized telemetry contracts from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. If subsystems emit raw, unstructured health data instead of normalized metrics, Safety Services will be forced to embed domain-specific parsing logic, diffusing DP ownership. MITIGATION: Establish and enforce telemetry schema contracts (e.g., ISO 13374 or custom health metric standards) at Telemetry interface. Safety Services consumes only contracted metrics, not raw sensor streams.

MODERATE RISK: Failover tasking (Tasking interface, bidirectional) may create tight coupling if Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems do not expose clean failover hooks. If failover logic is scattered across multiple subsystems (e.g., propulsion failover in Vehicle, link failover in Teleoperation), Safety Services becomes a coordinator of diffused DP logic rather than a centralized owner. MITIGATION: Require Vehicle and Teleoperation to expose standardized failover APIs (e.g., 'activate_redundant_propulsion', 'switch_teleoperation_link') that Safety Services invokes. Failover orchestration logic remains in Safety Services; subsystem-specific failover mechanics remain in respective modules.

LOW-TO-MODERATE RISK: Network Link dependency for distributed redundancy coordination introduces a potential single point of failure. If Safety Services relies on Network Link for all failover decisions, a network partition could prevent failover activation. MITIGATION: Ensure Safety Services includes local failover capability (e.g., watchdog timers, local health checks) that operates independently of Network Link. Network Link is used for coordination and diagnostics, not as a gating dependency for failover.

LOW RISK: Safety Services may grow into a monolithic module if availability architecture expands to include domain-specific redundancy strategies (e.g., Vehicle propulsion failover vs. Teleoperation link failover). MITIGATION: Decompose Safety Services into sub-modules (Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine, Recovery Orchestrator) with clear internal interfaces. Maintain Safety Services as the IM boundary; sub-modules are internal design artifacts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: EXCELLENT CONTAINMENT. Safety Services achieves a containment score of 0.92 with minimal diffusion (0.08). The module is the natural architectural home for integrated availability architecture because: (1) redundancy, fault tolerance, and rapid recovery are inherently safety-critical concerns aligned with Safety Services' domain; (2) predictive condition monitoring and diagnostics serve to prevent unsafe states; (3) the module already owns safety-related decision logic and alert generation; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert/Tasking out, Network Link for coordination) align cleanly with safety service responsibilities; (5) ownership is unambiguous and avoids diffusion across operational domains (Vehicle, Teleoperation) or top-level containers (NGHE Solution). Alternative placements (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) exhibit significantly lower containment scores (0.55, 0.40, 0.65 respectively) and higher diffusion risks due to cross-cutting nature of availability architecture.

recommended_revisions: REVISION 1 (Interface Specification): Formalize Telemetry interface contract. Define standardized health metric schema (e.g., subsystem_id, metric_type, value, timestamp, confidence_level) that Vehicle and Teleoperation must emit. Safety Services shall consume only contracted metrics; subsystem-specific parsing is prohibited. This prevents diffusion of DP logic into Safety Services.

REVISION 2 (Failover API): Establish standardized failover APIs for Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Define invocation signatures (e.g., 'activate_redundant_propulsion(subsystem_id, failover_mode)', 'switch_teleoperation_link(link_id, target_link)') that Safety Services uses to orchestrate failover. Subsystem-specific failover mechanics remain encapsulated within respective modules.

REVISION 3 (Local Failover Capability): Ensure Safety Services includes local failover capability (watchdog timers, local health checks) that operates independently of Network Link. Network Link is used for distributed coordination and remote diagnostics, not as a gating dependency for failover activation. This mitigates single point of failure risk.

REVISION 4 (Internal Decomposition): Decompose Safety Services into sub-modules (Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine, Recovery Orchestrator) with clear internal interfaces. Maintain Safety Services as the IM boundary; sub-modules are internal design artifacts. This prevents Safety Services from becoming monolithic as availability architecture expands.

REVISION 5 (Acceptance Criteria Operationalization): Define measurement protocol for availability metric (≥ 0.97). Specify: (1) measurement window (e.g., rolling 30-day period), (2) definition of 'operational' state (e.g., system capable of executing primary mission), (3) exclusions (e.g., scheduled maintenance, customer-initiated shutdowns), (4) data collection mechanism (e.g., Safety Services logs, external monitoring). This ensures verifiability against FR.

REVISION 6 (Conflict Resolution Protocol): Define priority rules and handoff protocols in Tasking interface specification to resolve potential conflicts between failover tasking and ongoing Vehicle or Teleoperation operations. Example: 'Safety-critical failover (e.g., propulsion redundancy activation) preempts non-critical operations (e.g., teleoperation commands).' This prevents ambiguity in recovery orchestration.

REVISION 7 (Network Partition Resilience): Document Safety Services' behavior during network partitions. Specify: (1) local failover thresholds (e.g., activate redundancy if health metric degrades below threshold for >5 seconds, regardless of Network Link status), (2) recovery protocol when network is restored (e.g., synchronize state with remote diagnostics), (3) logging of partition events for post-incident analysis. This ensures availability architecture is resilient to Network Link failures.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with strong technical coherence and excellent containment (0.92). However, the gate results expose seven critical interface and operational specification gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. Given HIGH safety criticality, these gaps represent unacceptable risk: (1) Telemetry interface lacks formalized health metric schema, risking diffusion of parsing logic into Safety Services; (2) Failover API contracts are undefined, creating tight coupling and scattered failover logic; (3) Network Link single-point-of-failure dependency is unmitigated; (4) Internal Safety Services decomposition strategy is not yet specified; (5) Availability metric operationalization (measurement window, 'operational' state definition, exclusions) lacks precision; (6) Conflict resolution between failover and ongoing operations is undefined; (7) Network partition resilience behavior is not documented. These are not design flaws but rather specification incompleteness at the DP-IM boundary. Revising the current level to incorporate REVISION 1-7 will harden the architecture before decomposing Safety Services into sub-modules (Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine, Recovery Orchestrator), which should be the next decomposition step. This approach ensures that sub-FR derivation is grounded in well-defined interface contracts and operational protocols.

revise.why: DP7 (Integrated availability architecture) is logically sound but operationally underspecified. Revisions must formalize: (1) Telemetry interface contract (standardized health metric schema); (2) Failover API specifications for Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; (3) Local failover capability independent of Network Link; (4) Internal decomposition strategy for Safety Services (Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine, Recovery Orchestrator); (5) Acceptance criteria operationalization (measurement window, 'operational' state, exclusions, data collection); (6) Conflict resolution protocol in Tasking interface; (7) Network partition resilience behavior. These revisions strengthen DP7 without changing its fundamental logic; they translate abstract 'integrated availability architecture' into concrete, verifiable design specifications. Once revised DP7 is locked, sub-FR decomposition can proceed with confidence that interface contracts and operational protocols are explicit.

assumptions: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can emit standardized health metrics conforming to a contracted schema (e.g., ISO 13374 or custom standard).

Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems can expose clean failover APIs (e.g., 'activate_redundant_propulsion', 'switch_teleoperation_link') without requiring Safety Services to embed domain-specific failover mechanics.
Safety Services can implement local failover capability (watchdog timers, local health checks) that operates independently of Network Link for at least critical subsystems (e.g., propulsion).
Network Link is available for distributed coordination and remote diagnostics but is not a gating dependency for failover activation.
Availability metric (≥ 0.97) can be measured over a defined evaluation period (e.g., rolling 30-day window) with clear definition of 'operational' state and documented exclusions.
Tasking interface can enforce priority rules (e.g., safety-critical failover preempts non-critical operations) without creating deadlock or race conditions.
Safety Services can detect and respond to network partitions within acceptable latency (e.g., <5 seconds) to activate local failover.
Internal decomposition of Safety Services into sub-modules (Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine, Recovery Orchestrator) does not introduce new coupling or diffusion risks.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Telemetry schema contract enforcement. If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems emit unstructured or non-compliant health data, Safety Services will be forced to embed parsing logic, diffusing DP ownership. Mitigation: Establish schema validation at Telemetry interface ingress; reject non-compliant data with logged alerts.

CRITICAL: Failover API adoption. If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems do not expose standardized failover APIs, Safety Services must coordinate scattered failover logic across subsystems, creating tight coupling and distributed DP ownership. Mitigation: Require failover API implementation as a contractual obligation; conduct interface compliance audits.

HIGH: Network Link single-point-of-failure. If Safety Services relies on Network Link for all failover decisions, a network partition could prevent failover activation, violating availability SLA. Mitigation: Implement local failover capability with independent health checks; document partition resilience behavior.

HIGH: Availability metric ambiguity. If 'operational availability ≥ 0.97' is not operationalized (measurement window, state definition, exclusions), verification will be subjective and non-repeatable. Mitigation: Formalize measurement protocol in acceptance criteria; establish baseline data collection before deployment.

MODERATE: Conflict resolution in Tasking interface. If failover tasking conflicts with ongoing Vehicle or Teleoperation operations, recovery orchestration may be delayed or blocked. Mitigation: Define priority rules and handoff protocols; conduct scenario testing.

MODERATE: Safety Services monolithic growth. If internal decomposition strategy is not specified, Safety Services may accumulate redundancy, monitoring, diagnostics, and orchestration logic without clear sub-module boundaries, reducing maintainability. Mitigation: Decompose into Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine, Recovery Orchestrator with explicit internal interfaces.

MODERATE: Network partition detection latency. If Safety Services cannot detect network partitions within acceptable latency (e.g., <5 seconds), failover activation may be delayed. Mitigation: Specify partition detection mechanism (e.g., heartbeat timeout, link status polling); validate latency in testing.
evidence_needed: Formalized Telemetry interface specification document defining health metric schema (subsystem_id, metric_type, value, timestamp, confidence_level, units), validation rules, and error handling.

Failover API specifications for Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, including invocation signatures, parameters, return codes, and expected behavior (e.g., 'activate_redundant_propulsion(subsystem_id, failover_mode) -> {status, activation_time, diagnostics}').
Safety Services architecture diagram showing local failover capability (watchdog timers, local health checks) that operates independently of Network Link, with latency and coverage specifications.

Internal decomposition design for Safety Services showing Redundancy Manager, Condition Monitor, Diagnostics Engine, Recovery Orchestrator sub-modules with clear internal interfaces and responsibility boundaries.

Operationalized acceptance criteria document specifying: (1) measurement window (e.g., rolling 30-day period), (2) definition of 'operational' state (e.g., system capable of executing primary mission with <X% performance degradation), (3) exclusions (e.g., scheduled maintenance, customer-initiated shutdowns), (4) data collection mechanism (e.g., Safety Services logs, external monitoring), (5) baseline availability target and tolerance.
Tasking interface specification document defining conflict resolution protocol, priority rules (e.g., safety-critical failover preempts non-critical operations), and handoff procedures.

Network partition resilience specification document defining: (1) local failover thresholds (e.g., activate redundancy if health metric degrades below threshold for >5 seconds, regardless of Network Link status), (2) recovery protocol when network is restored (e.g., synchronize state with remote diagnostics), (3) logging and alerting of partition events.

Test plan for availability architecture covering: (1) failover activation under nominal conditions, (2) failover activation during network partition, (3) recovery after network restoration, (4) predictive anomaly detection accuracy, (5) end-to-end availability measurement over evaluation period.
